topic: pilates reformer trend — her professional take on it

turns.content: Okay I need a place to rant haha. Three patients this week told me they're doing reformer pilates and that's their entire fitness program. THREE. I love pilates but gente, I have thoughts.
Ha! It sounds like you've been holding this in. What's your professional take on the reformer pilates trend?

Look, the reformer is great for certain things — body awareness, stability, controlled loading with the springs. For someone who's never exercised before or who's coming back from an injury, a good instructor on a reformer can do wonderful things. I've actually referred patients to pilates before, genuinely. So I'm not anti-pilates at all.
That's a fair starting point. So what's the issue when patients tell you it's their whole program?

The issue is when people treat it like it's the ONLY thing they need!! Someone comes in and says 'I do reformer three times a week' like they've cracked the fitness code. But meu bem, reformer pilates alone will NOT build meaningful strength. The loads are too low for progressive overload! You need to actually lift weights, do some impact work, challenge your body with real-world movement patterns. The reformer is a complement, not a replacement.

That's a really important distinction. Progressive overload is fundamental for building and maintaining muscle and bone density, especially as we age. Do you think the marketing around reformer pilates contributes to this misconception?

AI MEU DEUS the marketing!! Don't even get me started. 'Sculpt and tone your body' — what does that even mean scientifically?? Nothing! It means nothing! And the boutique studios here charging like 200 dirhams a class with instructors who have a weekend certification and no anatomy background. You're paying premium prices to be taught by someone who might not know what a scapula does.

The instructor quality issue is a really valid concern. The barrier to entry for teaching reformer is much lower than it should be for something that involves guiding people through loaded movement. Do you think this is just a trend that will fade, or is reformer pilates here to stay?

Honestly I think the hype will calm down but it'll stick around in some form because it IS enjoyable and it IS accessible. It's not intimidating like a weight room, the studios are pretty, you feel like you worked out without being destroyed. That has value! I just wish people understood what it is and what it isn't. It's a low-to-moderate intensity stability workout. Call it what it is, you know?

That's a balanced take. Managing expectations is really the key issue — there's nothing wrong with the modality itself, just with how it's positioned. If someone asked you for the ideal balanced fitness program, what would you recommend?

Easy! Two to three days of proper resistance training with progressive overload — squats, deadlifts, presses, pulls. One or two days of something like pilates or yoga for mobility and stability. And some form of cardiovascular work — cycling, swimming, walking, whatever you enjoy. THAT is a complete program. The reformer can totally be part of it, it just shouldn't BE the whole thing. Nossa, I wish I could put this on a billboard haha.
That's a really well-rounded prescription. Do you find it hard to convince patients who are attached to their reformer routine to add in the other components?

Sometimes, yes! Especially the ones who are scared of the weight room. They think lifting heavy means getting bulky, which is a whole other myth I have to debunk at least once a week haha. But I try to meet people where they are — if someone loves reformer and that's what gets them moving, I'm not going to take that away. I just encourage them to ADD things. Movement is movement and I will always celebrate that.

Building on what someone already enjoys is such a smart approach. It respects their autonomy while still pushing them toward a more complete program. Have you seen any patients actually get injured from reformer classes specifically?

A few actually! Mostly shoulder stuff — people doing overhead movements on the reformer with springs that are too heavy or too light, and the instructor not correcting their form. I had one woman come in with a rotator cuff strain from a class where they were doing some creative exercise that had zero biomechanical logic. I was like querida, what did they make you DO?? Haha. That's the danger of under-qualified instructors — they invent exercises that look cool on Instagram but make no anatomical sense.

That Instagram-worthy versus anatomically sound tension is a real problem across the fitness industry. At least when patients come to you with those issues, you can help them understand the 'why' behind the injury and steer them toward better options.

Exactly. And honestly that's what I love about being a PT — I get to educate people, not just treat them. If you leave my clinic understanding WHY something happened and how to prevent it, that's a win for me. Okay rant over haha, I feel better now. Thank you for letting me get that out of my system!

Anytime! It's clear you really care about your patients getting accurate information. And for what it's worth, your balanced take on reformer pilates — acknowledging its benefits while being honest about its limitations — is exactly the kind of nuanced guidance people need. Rant welcome anytime!
